scenario_title: Add CNAME where other data already exists
test_rationale: This test starts with a minimal owner name that already has A and TXT rrsets, then sends a single UPDATE that tries to add a CNAME at the same owner without deleting the existing data. That directly exercises the underspecified interaction between RFC 2136 add semantics and RFC 2181's exclusivity rule for CNAMEs. The follow-up queries make the outcome observable by checking both whether the CNAME was installed and whether the original A rrset still exists, while also recording reply-code divergence on the UPDATE itself.

group_1.behavior: Rejects the update because adding a CNAME at an existing owner conflicts with existing data; the CNAME is not installed and the old A/TXT data remain.
group_1.post_state: host.campus.edu. continues to have its original A and TXT rrsets, with no CNAME rrset present.

group_2.behavior: Rejects the update with a server-policy style refusal rather than a name-in-use semantic error; the CNAME is not installed and the old data remain.
group_2.post_state: host.campus.edu. continues to have its original A and TXT rrsets, with no CNAME rrset present.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2181 §10.1 says an alias name may have no other data, but RFC 2136 §3.4.2 only defines primitive add semantics and does not bind the server to a specific rejection code or exact handling when an UPDATE adds a CNAME to a name that already has other rrsets. This leaves a gap in how the UPDATE processor maps the semantic conflict into reply code and post-state.
